elements.sent-2411-001: We derive an exact solution for a simple non-autonomous delay differential equation (DDE) over the entire real-time axis, representing it as a sum of Gaussian-shaped dynamics with distinct peak positions.
elements.sent-2411-002: This marks the first explicit solution for non-autonomous DDEs and is a rare example even among general DDEs.
elements.sent-2411-003: The constructed solution offers key physical insights and facilitates the analysis of system properties, such as the envelope profile of the dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-004: In the study of dynamics with time delays, quite complex behaviors have been discovered.
elements.sent-2411-005: Time delays most commonly arise due to finite transmission and production times.
elements.sent-2411-006: Time delay is an intrinsic feature in many control and interaction systems and has been studied across various fields, such as mathematics, biology, physics, engineering, and economics (e.g., \cite{heiden1979, bellman1963, cabrera_1, hayes1950, insperger, kcuhler, longtinmilton1989a, mackeyglass1977, miltonetal2009b, ohirayamane2000, smith2010, stepan1989, stepaninsperger, szydlowski2010 ).}
elements.sent-2411-007: In general, delays induce instability in stable fixed points, leading to oscillatory and more complex dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-008: Additionally, as the delay lengthens, the complexity of the dynamics increases.
elements.sent-2411-009: For example, the Mackey-Glass equation \cite{mackeyglass1977 shows a progression from monotonic convergence to transient oscillations, sustained oscillations, and finally chaotic dynamics as the delay parameter of the feedback function increases.}
elements.sent-2411-010: With models like this, the pathway to complex behavior in many delay systems is gradually becoming understood (e.g., \cite{taylor ), but due to its complexity, there is still much room for further investigation in this field.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Recently, we have been studying delay differential equations, focusing specifically on a category known as non-autonomous DDEs, where the coefficients vary with time.
elements.sent-2411-012: These equations are generally considered more challenging to analyze than autonomous DDEs and are primarily investigated using approximate or numerical methods \cite{Busenberg1984,Ming1990,Ford2002,Gyori2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: In particular, we have been examining the following simple non-autonomous delay differential equation, where $a$, $b$ are real constants and the delay $\tau \geq 0$ \cite{kentaohira2022,kentaohira2023,kentaohira2024 : \begin{equation}\label{DDE1} {dX(t)\over dt} + a t X(t) = b X(t-\tau)\end{equation} This equation represents a slight extension of Hayes' equation, which has been extensively studied \cite{hayes1950 .}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{DDE1} {dX(t)\over dt} + a t X(t) = b X(t-\tau)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-014: Hayes' equation is given as follows, where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are real constants. \begin{equation}{dX(t)\over dt} + \alpha X(t) = \beta X(t-\tau) \label{Hayes}\end{equation} The difference between equations (\ref{DDE1 ) and (\ref{Hayes ) lies in the second term, which becomes a linear function of time in equation (1).}}
elements.sent-2411-015: While this modification may seem minor, it leads to significantly different behavior.
elements.sent-2411-016: Notably, we have observed that the dynamics of equation (\ref{DDE1 ) exhibit a frequency resonance phenomenon\cite{kentaohira2022,kentaohira2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: In this paper, we focus on the non-autonomous DDE described by equation (\ref{DDE1 ) and demonstrate that its exact solution over the entire real time axis can be explicitly expressed as a sum of Gaussian-shaped dynamics with different peak positions.}
elements.sent-2411-018: To the best of the author's knowledge, this is the first instance where such an explicit solution has been obtained for non-autonomous DDEs.
elements.sent-2411-019: Moreover, it is a rare result even within the domain of DDEs in general.
elements.sent-2411-020: The constructed solution provides valuable physical insights and enables us to explain several properties of the system, such as the envelope profile of its dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-021: For the differential equation (\ref{DDE1 ), if $a > 0$, then as $t \rightarrow \infty$ or $t \rightarrow -\infty$, $X(t)$ converges to 0.}
elements.sent-2411-023: At $t \rightarrow \infty$, we can approximate $bX(t-\tau) - a t X(t) \approx - a t X(t)$, so the differential equation near $t \rightarrow \infty$ becomes \begin{equation}{dX(t)\over dt} = -a t X(t)\end{equation} which has the following solution: \begin{equation}X(t) = {\cal{C}}e^{-{1 \over 2}a t^2}\end{equation} For $a > 0$, this converges to 0, and for $a < 0$, it diverges.
elements.sent-2411-024: A similar approach can be applied for $t \rightarrow -\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-025: In other words, if $a > 0$, $X(t)$ is globally asymptotically stable at $X = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-027: When $a = 0$, the original differential equation becomes: \begin{equation}{dX(t)\over dt} = b X(t-\tau) \label{DDE1_Hayes_special_case}\end{equation} which is the same as Hayes' equation (\ref{Hayes ) with $\alpha = 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-028: It is known that for this equation, $X = 0$ is globally asymptotically stable when $- \pi / {2 \tau < b < 0$, and unstable outside of this range.}
elements.sent-2411-029: In the following sections, we apply the Fourier transform to the differential equation (\ref{DDE1 ) and subsequently perform an inverse transform\cite{kentaohira2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Based on the properties of the transform, the function obtained through this process coincides with the solution to the differential equation (\ref{DDE1 ) over \( t \in \mathbb{R \).}}
elements.sent-2411-031: In the following, we regard that \( X(t) \) is not Fourier transformable if it can only be transformed when it is the zero function.
elements.sent-2411-032: When \( a < 0 \), the function is not Fourier transformable.
elements.sent-2411-033: As shown in the previous section, \( X(t) \) diverges as \( t \rightarrow \infty \) and \( t \rightarrow -\infty \) for \( a < 0 \).
elements.sent-2411-034: Consequently, \( X(t) \) is not absolutely integrable over \( t \in \mathbb{R \), making it non-transformable by Fourier transform.}
elements.sent-2411-035: In this scenario, the original differential equation is given by: \begin{equation}\frac{dX(t)}{dt} = b X(t - \tau)\end{equation} The general solution for this is known to be expressed via the Lambert \( W \) function as follows \cite{shinozaki, pusenjak2017 : \begin{equation}X(t) = \sum_{k = -\infty}^{\infty} C_k e^{\lambda_k t}, \quad \lambda_k = \frac{1}{\tau} W_k(b \tau)\end{equation} Here, \( C_k \) are constants that satisfy \( \text{Im (X(t)) = 0 \).}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Thus, for \( X(t) \) to be absolutely integrable, each \( C_k \) must equal zero, which implies that \( X(t) \) cannot be Fourier transformed unless it is the zero function.
elements.sent-2411-037: In the previous section, it was shown that for \( a > 0 \), \( X(t) \) converges to zero as \( t \rightarrow \infty \) and \( t \rightarrow -\infty \), meaning \( X(t) \) is absolutely integrable over \( t \in \mathbb{R \) and can be Fourier transformed.}
elements.subsection-2411-002: Derivation of the General Solution in Integral Form
elements.sent-2411-038: In the parameter range where Fourier transformability is possible (\( a > 0 \)), we seek the general solution to the delay differential equation (\ref{DDE1 ) over \( t \in \mathbb{R \).}}
elements.sent-2411-039: For \( \tau > 0 \), applying the Fourier transform to equation (\ref{DDE1 ) gives a differential equation for \( \hat{X (\omega) \): \begin{equation}i \omega \hat{X}(\omega) + i a \frac{d \hat{X}(\omega)}{d \omega} = b e^{-i \tau \omega} \hat{X}(\omega)\end{equation} Solving this equation provides: \begin{equation}\hat{X}(\omega) = {\cal{C}} \exp \left( -\frac{1}{2a} \omega^2 + \frac{b}{a \tau} e^{-i \tau \omega} \right)\end{equation} By taking the inverse Fourier transform of \( \hat{X (\omega) \), we obtain the general solution \( X(t) \) for the original differential equation: \begin{equation}X(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \hat{X}(\omega) e^{i t \omega} d\omega = \frac{{\cal{C}}}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \exp \left( -\frac{1}{2a} \omega^2 + \frac{b}{a \tau} e^{-i \tau \omega} + i t \omega \right) d\omega\end{equation} This function \( X(t) \) satisfies the original differential equation (\ref{DDE1 ) for any \( t \in \mathbb{R \).}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-040: In order to investigate the above solution, we proceed with additional calculations by completing the square for the terms involving \( \omega^2 \) and \( \omega \): \begin{equation}X(t) = \frac{{\cal{C}}}{2\pi} e^{-\frac{1}{2}a t^2} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \exp \left( -\frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{\omega}{\sqrt{a}} - i \sqrt{a} t \right)^2 + \frac{b}{a \tau} e^{a \tau t - i \sqrt{a} \tau s} \right) d\omega\end{equation} Using a variable change \( s = \frac{\omega {\sqrt{a - i \sqrt{a t \), we rewrite: \begin{equation}X(t) = {\frac{{\cal{C}}}{2\pi}} \sqrt{a} e^{-\frac{1}{2}a t^2} \int_{-\infty - i \sqrt{a} t}^{\infty - i \sqrt{a} t} \exp \left( -\frac{1}{2} s^2 + \frac{b}{a \tau} e^{a \tau t - i \sqrt{a} \tau s} \right) ds \label{DDE1complex_integral_t=0}\end{equation} Let \( g(s) \) denote the integrand, then \begin{equation}X(t)={\frac{{\cal{C}}}{2\pi}}\sqrt{a}e^{-{1\over 2}at^2}\int_{-\infty-i\sqrt{a}t}^{\infty-i\sqrt{a}t}g(s)ds.\end{equation}.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-052: While \( f(x) \) cannot be expressed using elementary functions, we can determine \( f^{(n) (0) \) using it: \begin{equation} f'(x) = \beta f(\alpha x), \quad f'(0) = \beta f(0) f''(x) = \alpha \beta f'(\alpha x) = \alpha \beta^2 f(\alpha^2 x), \quad f''(0) = \alpha \beta^2 f(0) f^{(3)}(x) = \alpha^2 \beta f''(\alpha x) = \alpha^3 \beta^3 f(\alpha^3 x), \quad f^{(3)}(0) = \alpha^3 \beta^3 f(0) f^{(4)}(x) = \alpha^3 \beta f^{(3)}(\alpha x) = \alpha^6 \beta^4 f(\alpha^4 x), \quad f^{(4)}(0) = \alpha^6 \beta^4 f(0) \dots \quad \dots \quad \dots \dots \quad \dots \quad \dots f^{(n)}(x) = \alpha^{\frac{n(n-1)}{2}} \beta^n f(\alpha^n x), \quad f^{(n)}(0) = \alpha^{\frac{n(n-1)}{2}} \beta^n f(0) \end{equation} Thus, setting \( {\cal{C = f(0) \), substituting values for \( \alpha \) and \( \beta \), and rearranging, \( f(x) \) can be expanded at \( x = 0 \) as follows: \begin{equation}f(x) = {\cal{C}} \sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n!} \left( \frac{b}{a \tau} \right)^n \left( e^{-{1 \over 2} a \tau^2} \right)^{n^2} x^n\end{equation} The convergence of this series is demonstrated by the Cauchy-Hadamard theorem.}}}

elements.section-2411-005: Summary of General Solutions and Discussion
elements.sent-2411-073: To summarize, the solutions of the differential equation \begin{equation}\frac{dX(t)}{dt} + a t X(t) = b X(t-\tau),\quad t \in \mathbb{R},\quad a,b,\tau \in \mathbb{R}\end{equation} can be categorized as follows: \vskip\baselineskip (i) For \( a = 0 \): \begin{equation}X(t) = \sum_{k = -\infty}^{\infty} C_k e^{\lambda_k t}, \quad \lambda_k = \frac{1}{\tau} W_k (b \tau)\end{equation} where \( C_k \) satisfies \( \operatorname{Im (X(t)) = 0 \).}
elements.formula-2411-039: \begin{equation}\frac{dX(t)}{dt} + a t X(t) = b X(t-\tau),\quad t \in R,\quad a,b,\tau \in R\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-074: However, as \( |t| \rightarrow \infty \), it diverges similarly to \( e^{-\frac{1 {2 a t^2 \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-075: Our constructed solution is valid over the entire real-time axis, as mentioned.
elements.sent-2411-076: Given the uniqueness of the solution for first-order equations and the general unique correspondence of Fourier transform pairs, we believe that this solution is unique up to a scaling factor.
elements.sent-2411-077: However, a rigorous mathematical proof of this claim is still required.
elements.sent-2411-078: In the typical formulation of DDEs, the initial function is specified for $t \in [-\tau, 0]$, and the solution is then sought for $t > 0$.
elements.sent-2411-079: When the initial function over $t \in [- \tau, 0]$ does not deviate significantly from the constructed solution, our result can serve as an approximation for such initial-value problems (see \cite{kentaohira2024 for details).}
elements.sent-2411-080: Overall, we hope that our exact solution for a non-autonomous DDE provides further insights into the nontrivial behaviors induced by delays.

elements.sent-2411-090: For \( \tau > 0 \), applying the Fourier transform to equation (\ref{DDE1 ) gives a differential equation for \( \hat{X (\omega) \): \begin{equation}i \omega \hat{X}(\omega) + i a \frac{d \hat{X}(\omega)}{d \omega} = b e^{-i \tau \omega} \hat{X}(\omega)\end{equation} Solving this equation provides: \begin{equation}\hat{X}(\omega) = {\cal{C}} \exp \left[ -\frac{1}{2a} \omega^2 + \frac{b}{a \tau} e^{-i \tau \omega} \right]\end{equation} By taking the inverse Fourier transform of \( \hat{X (\omega) \), we obtain the general solution \( X(t) \) for the original differential equation: \begin{equation}X(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \hat{X}(\omega) e^{i t \omega} d\omega = \frac{{\cal{C}}}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \exp \left[ -\frac{1}{2a} \omega^2 + \frac{b}{a \tau} e^{-i \tau \omega} + i t \omega \right] d\omega\end{equation} This function \( X(t) \) satisfies the original differential equation (\ref{DDE1 ) for any \( t \in \mathbb{R \).}}}}}
